data.title: Software sector plunges, cybersecurity names hit hardest, as AI disruption concerns persist
data.description:
Software companies nearly across the board were bleeding on Thursday, with cybersecurity names hit the hardest amid persistent fears of AI disruption and a shaky geopolitical backdrop.

Cybersecurity firms were hemorrhaging the most, led by Qualys (QLYS), which was down 13% by afternoon trading. Cloudflare (NET), Tenable (TENB), and Zscaler (ZS) had all tumbled about 11%.  

Earlier this week, Anthropic (ANTHRO) announced Claude Mythos, its most powerful model, which it said was capable of identifying hidden cybersecurity issues across a broad range of software systems. Anthropic has only shared the model with about 40 tech giants, including cybersecurity stalwarts such as CrowdStrike (CRWD) and Palo Alto Networks (PANW).

"If Mythos is that strong and that powerful and it's exposing these vulnerabilities that have been around for years, it just shows one, the weakness of the current software that's out there, and two, that AI ​is ​still making incredible progress versus legacy software companies," said Michael O'Rourke, ​chief market strategist at JonesTrading, according to Reuters.

Moreover, OpenAI (OPENAI) is also finalizing a new, powerful model with advanced cybersecurity capabilities that it plans to release only to a small set of companies, similar to Anthropic's limited rollout of Mythos.

The bleeding was apparent across the entire software sector as the iShares Expanded Tech-Software Sector ETF (IGV) had dropped nearly 5%. It is down 28% year to date. 

In contrast, all the major indices, including the Dow Jones Industrial Average Index (DJI), the S&P 500 Index (SP500), and the NASDAQ Composite Index (COMP:IND), were all up about half a percent. Semiconductor and hardware stocks were also unaffected by the carnage, as the Philadelphia Semiconductor Index (SOX) had edged up 1.5%.

Enterprise software was also taking hits. ServiceNow (NOW) and Appian (APPN) had both sunk 8%, Oracle (ORCL) had fallen 5%, and Salesforce (CRM) had dipped by 3.6%. 

Samsara (IOT) had tanked by 9%, and Intuit (INTU) and Autodesk (ADSK) had both plummeted 8%. HubSpot (HUBS) was down 7%.

Palantir (PLTR) had also plunged by 8%. After being immune to much of the software selloff during 2025, its shares have now declined 27% year to date, well off its all-time high of $200 reached in late October.

data.title: Large-cap REITs ranked by quant ratings ahead of Q1 earnings
data.description:
The Real Estate Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (XLRE) had a steady start to 2026. In Q1, it gained around 1.11%, outperforming the S&P 500, which fell by about 4.81%. This shows that REIT stocks stayed relatively strong even when the broader market struggled.

Looking at three-month data, XLRE moved up from $40.44 to $42.44, giving a gain of ~4.95%, reported the State Street Investment Management tracker. The rise was slow but steady, showing that investors are still comfortable holding real estate assets.

At the same time, the 10-year Treasury yield also climbed to around 4.30%. Higher rates usually make borrowing expensive, which can affect real estate demand and valuations.

According to the Nareit report, private real estate valuations are not adjusting quickly. This delay is making it harder to understand the true value of assets and reducing market liquidity. But on the positive side, if the valuation gap closes, REITs could perform even better.

Below are the SA-reported top 5 large-cap REIT stocks with market capitalizations exceeding $10B, highlighting those with the highest and lowest quant ratings ahead of Q1 2026 results.

Highest rated:

  • Sun Hung Kai Properties (SUHJY), Quant rating: 4.88, Strong Buy.
  • Jones Lang LaSalle (JLL), Quant rating: 4.72, Strong Buy.
  • W.P. Carey (WPC), Quant rating: 4.66, Strong Buy.
  • Regency Centers (REG), Quant rating: 4.55, Strong Buy.
  • VICI Properties (VICI), Quant rating: 4.50, Strong Buy.

Lowest rated:

  • Crown Castle (CCI), Quant rating: 1.08, Strong Sell.
  • SBA Communications (SBAC), Quant rating: 1.30, Strong Sell.
  • Weyerhaeuser (WY), Quant rating: 1.38, Strong Sell.
  • American Tower (AMT), Quant rating: 1.72, Sell.
  • Iron Mountain (IRM), Quant Rating: 1.88, Sell.

REIT ETFs: iShares U.S. Real Estate ETF (IYR), Vanguard Real Estate ETF (VNQ), iShares Mortgage Real Estate ETF (REM), Vanguard Global ex-US Real Estate Index Fd ETF (VNQI), iShares Residential and Multisector Real Estate ETF (REZ)

data.title: Berkshire brokerage must face commission lawsuit despite settlement, judge rules - report
data.description:
A federal judge ruled that Berkshire Hathaway's (BRK.B) (BRK.A) HomeServices of America brokerage unit must face a proposed antitrust suit that alleges it conspired to inflate commissions that home sellers pay real estate agents, according to a media report on Thursday. 

The judge said the brokerage's $250M settlement for the same claims in 2024 doesn't protect the company from the new suit, Reuters reported. The HomeServices settlement was separate from a $418M settlement reached between plaintiffs and the National Association of Realtors ((NAR)).

U.S. District Judge Stephen Bough in Kansas City, Missouri, rejected Berkshire Hathaway Energy's argument that the alleged dealings with HomeServices qualified as a “single enterprise” for antitrust purposes. Under BHE's reasoning, the company didn't owe additional damages to home sellers due to the settlement.

The NAR changed its real estate commission rules after a jury in 2023 ordered the trade group and four brokerages to pay $1.8B to home sellers who said they were overcharged. The defendants later settled, but some objectors, which included home buyers and sellers, asked the 8th U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals to void the settlement as inadequate. Oral arguments were held in January, Reuters said.

Despite the ruling, class B shares in Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B), which is better known for its insurance businesses, rose 1.4% in Thursday afternoon trading.

data.title: Musk reshapes xAI under SpaceX as leadership shake-up signals AI catch-up push
data.description:
Elon Musk’s artificial intelligence venture, xAI, is undergoing a sweeping restructuring as it integrates more closely with SpaceX ahead of a potential blockbuster IPO, Business Insider reported Thursday.

A newly appointed president, Michael Nicholls, previously a senior SpaceX (SPACE) executive, told staff the company is lagging competitors and must accelerate improvements. The reorganization reflects a broader effort to rebuild xAI’s engineering and product operations while narrowing the gap with rivals like OpenAI, Anthropic and Google.

The overhaul includes new leadership across key AI development stages. Devendra Chaplot will oversee model pre-training, Aman Madaan will manage infrastructure and tooling, and Aditya Gupta will lead post-training and reinforcement learning. Additional hires will guide coding models, as well as image and video systems.

On the product side, recent hires Andrew Milich and Jason Ginsburg will lead development of xAI’s Grok suite, while infrastructure roles are increasingly being filled by former SpaceX (SPACE) engineers. Nicholls also flagged weak computing efficiency as a major issue, with plans to improve performance quickly.

The changes come amid significant turnover. Several cofounders and senior leaders have exited in recent months, and the company has cut teams tied to certain AI tools and recruiting. Since its acquisition earlier this year, xAI has seen frequent internal reshuffling, with Musk at times overseeing a large number of direct reports.

The restructuring follows Musk’s acknowledgment that the company’s initial build was flawed, prompting a ground-up reset as it pushes toward a high-stakes public offering that could value the combined business in the trillions.

data.title: Kosmos Energy cut to Sell at Goldman after strong 2026 performance
data.description:
Kosmos Energy (KOS) down 7% in Thursday's trading as Goldman Sachs downgraded shares to Sell from Neutral with a $22 price target, following 200% gains YTD - albeit off a low base - making the stock the best performing story in the XOP, driven by three factors, analyst Neil Mehta said.

Kosmos (KOS) volumes have improved in Mauritania and Senegal after a challenging start-up at Tortue; the company still has significant oil beta and investors have raised mid-cycle Brent expectations closer to Goldman's mid-cycle outlook of $75/bbl, based on dialogue; and Kosmos has taken steps to reduce debt—but after sharp outperformance, Mehta said he sees less upside than for large-cap peers such as ConocoPhillips (COP) and mid-cap names such as Ovintiv (OVV), Permian Resources (PR), and Viper Energy (VNOM).

Mehta also said he views quarterly and operational execution as a key risk for Kosmos (KOS) in light of past project delays, cost overruns, and negative guidance revisions, and the analyst expects a more conservative production outlook for Jubilee results due to a history of operational challenges over prior quarters relating to riser instability, FPSO downtime,
insufficient water injection, and power reliability issues.

data.title: Brown-Forman could see $33-$34 a share in takeover - analyst
data.description:
Brown-Forman (BF.A) (BF.B) could see $33-$34 a share in a potential sale after a report that Southern Comfort whiskey maker Sazerac is competing with France's Pernod Ricard (PDRDF) for the spirits company, according to a TD Cowen analyst.

"Given the scarce-asset nature of the Brown-Forman business and its biggest brand, Jack Daniel's, we think a buyer would have to pay a 30%-35% equity premium to get a deal done with complete change of control," TD Cowen analyst Robert Moskow wrote in a note on Thursday. 

Shares of Brown-Forman (BF. A) (BF. B) jumped 16% after a report that  Sazerac recently approached the maker of Jack Daniel's about a potential acquisition, according to a WSJ report on Thursday, which cited people familiar with the matter.

TD Cowen's Moskow said he wouldn't be surprised to see interest for Brown-Forman (BF. B) from other distilled spirits companies. Bacardi and Campari (DVDCF) could emerge as other possible bidders as well as Diageo (DEO), though Diageo is highly leveraged. 

Antitrust may be an issue for a potential Sazerac combination with Brown-Forman (BF.B) as it would constitute 18% of the market and nearly 40% of the American whiskey share, according to Moskow.

data.title: Comcast's Xfinity adds Disney+, Hulu, and HBO Max to bundle
data.description:
• Comcast’s Xfinity (CMCSA) launched a major expansion of its StreamSaver bundle, now offering Disney+ (DIS), Hulu Bundle (DIS), and HBO Max (WBD) in addition to its current offerings that include Peacock (CMCSA), Netflix (NFLX) with ads, and Apple TV (AAPL).
  • Subscribers can choose between the additional channels/streaming services or select one of eight available bundles featuring combinations of the above, included with Comcast’s Xfinity service.
  • Xfinity will continue to expand StreamStore’s offerings with additional streaming services and other options through bundled and a la carte selections.

data.title: Utility stocks Ameren, Evergy started with Buy ratings at BTIG
data.description:
Ameren (AEE) up 1.8% and Evergy (EVRG) up 1.3% in Thursday's trading as BTIG Research initiates coverage of both utility stocks with Buy ratings and respective $131 and $99 price targets.

BTIG believes Ameren (AEE) is on the verge of seeing notable growth in Missouri, as the state has become increasingly attractive to large load customers, particularly data centers and manufacturing facilities, and the progression of the Missouri regulatory front into one of the most
constructive jurisdictions continues to support the company's growth.

With Ameren (AEE) showing increased transparency around anticipated demand additions and visibility on new electric service agreements and projects, BTIG believes the company is well positioned for 6%-8% earnings per share growth into the next decade with strong potential for upside.

Evergy (EVRG) has taken steps toward an elevated growth story after recent years of rather muted growth, BTIG analysts said, seeing the company's current 6%-8%-plus EPS growth outlook as reasonable and potentially conservative given the potential for additional large load, warranting a reversion toward premium valuation.

The company's relatively small size also implies enhanced leverage to large load growth potential, BTIG said, believing above-average growth potential, potential upside, and improving regulatory environments suggest the stock can re-rate higher.

data.title: Prediction markets point to Iran de-escalation as Wall Street extends gains
data.description:
Wall Street’s major indexes advanced Thursday afternoon as investors weighed evolving developments surrounding a conditional ceasefire between the United States and Iran.

Market participants are increasingly focused on the timeline for a potential de-escalation in the Middle East, with uncertainty around when military operations may conclude continuing to influence sentiment. 

Prediction markets are offering additional insight into expectations. Traders on Polymarket are currently assigning just over a 50% probability that strikes on Iran will end before the month’s close, reflecting cautious optimism but lingering uncertainty.

See what Polymarket traders are forecasting when asked when Trump will announce an end to military operations against Iran. 

  • By April 15—15% chance.
  • By April 21—29% chance.
  • By April 30—53% chance.
  • By May 31—71% chance.
  • By June 30—80% chance.

Market Tracking ETFs: (DIA), (DDM), (DOG), (DXD), (SDOW), (SPY), (VOO), (IVV), (RSP), (SSO), (UPRO), (SH), (SDS), (SPXU), (QQQ), (QQQM), (TQQQ), (QID), and (SQQQ).

data.title: Howard Marks outlines growing concerns about private credit in new memo
data.description:
In a memo to Oaktree clients Thursday, veteran investor Howard Marks outlined growing concerns about the private credit (VPC) market, particularly direct lending, as the sector faces its first major test since the 2008 Global Financial Crisis.

Marks traces the evolution of credit markets from the late 1970s through today, noting how direct lending exploded over the past 15 years as banks pulled back from risky lending. The sector has ballooned from roughly $150B two decades ago to about $2T in direct loans.

“I imagine some direct lending managers accepted too much money and invested it too fast, applying standards that were too low and setting the scene for a correction,” Marks wrote.

The memo highlights particular concerns about software (IGV) company debt, which represents 20-30% of direct lending portfolios. Recent advances in artificial intelligence—including Anthropic’s (ANTHRO) powerful new coding model released in late 2025—have raised questions about the long-term viability of many software businesses that borrowed heavily during the boom years.

Marks points to recent bankruptcies at First Brands and Tricolor, which “caught credit investors by surprise” and raised concerns about possible fraud. Some investors in non-traded business development companies have faced redemption limits when attempting to withdraw their money. Meanwhile, investors sought to get back more than $20B of their investments from private credit firms in Q1 2026

“Investors initially fall in love with the new thing, swallow its promises whole, and overpay,” Marks observed. “Then, when disappointment and disillusionment set in, the bravado and confidence that originally supported the investment evaporate.”

Oaktree has positioned itself defensively, with direct lending comprising less than 15% of overall assets under management. Marks noted that the firm’s software exposure is “substantially less than that of our peers.”

Drawing parallels to the high-yield bond (HYG) market turmoil of the early 1990s, Marks concluded that direct lending will ultimately prove sound but “may have to go through a credit cycle to get to a better place.”
